coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of carbon dots with tailored heteroatomic structure for achieving ultrahigh effectiveness in persulfate activation
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Pei
Zhu, Ziqi
Tang, Ruixin
Shen, An
Huang, Wentao
Zhou, Xiaoyan
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are normally feature with zero-dimensional structure, excellent solubility, good biosafety, and exceptional photoelectronic properties, thus, awakening the homogeneous-like photocatalytic potency of CDs in peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation can afford new idea for water body remediation. Herein, we presented a facile nitrogen and sulfur co-doping strategy for the development of high-performance CDs-based photocatalysts. Through the deep investigation on the structure-activity relationship, we proposed that the incorporated pyridinic N within CDs structure could serve as efficient Lewis basic sites for PMS confinement. Importantly, the co-existence of oxidized sulfur groups and specific nitrogen speciation (pyridine N and pyrrolic N) induced unique “push-pull” effect on the photogenerated carriers within the surface state of S,N co-doped CDs. The unique synergy sites and surface hydrophilic nature conferred the CDs exceptional photocatalytic effectiveness, presenting a remarkable PMS consumption ratio of 7.62 per gram of the CDs photocatalyst within just 20 min. Profited from the improved kinetics of interfacial reactions, the CDs-photocatalyzed oxidation system that consisted largely of sulfate radicals can completely degrade rhodamine B within 12.5 min, and hold great potential in long-term operation without needing to regenerate CDs catalyst.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Heteroatom speciation
Photocatalytic effectiveness
Peroxymonosulfate activation
Wastewater remediation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphologies and surface chemical states of heteroatoms-doped CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Postdoctoral Research Funding Program of Jiangsu Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [32271784, 32101460], the Postdoctoral Science Foundation of China [2020M681624], the Postdoctoral Research Funding Program of Jiangsu Province [2021K042A], and the Advanced Analysis and Testing Center of Nanjing Forestry University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) S,N-CDs1.0, (b) S,N-CDs0.5 and (c) N-CDs with histograms of the corresponding particle size distribution.

(a) XRD patterns and (b) FTIR spectra of S,N-CDs1.0, S,N-CDs0.5 and N-CDs. (c) The XPS full survey spectra showing the primary elemental compositions of heteroatoms-doped CDs. The high resolution XPS of (d) C 1s, (e) N 1s, (f) S 2p and (g) O 1s spectra of the synthesized CDs.

PL emission spectra of (a) S,N-CDs1.0, (b) S,N-CDs0.5 and (c) N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (d) The normalized optimal emission spectra, and (e) normalized absorption spectra of three CD samples. (f) PL decay curves of the synthesized CDs excited at 370 nm.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of PMS/KI and the synthesized CDs in the wavelength range of 300∼500 nm, revealing that the measurement for PMS decay in oxidation system was free from the interference of CDs-based catalyst. (b) The dependence of PMS consumption efficiency on the reaction time in different CDs photoactivated PMS systems. (c) The effectiveness of different carbocatalysts in PMS activation. (d) The effects of various quenching agents on the removal efficiency of RhB in S,N-CDs1.0 photoactivated PMS system. (e) EPR spectra of DMPO-SO4 •− and DMPO-OH• in S,N-CDs1.0 photoactivated PMS system recorded at different reaction time. (f) Investigation on the photostability of S,N-CDs1.0 by continuous irradiation using Xe lamp (blue line); Reusability of S,N-CDs1.0 for RhB degradation in the photocatalytic oxidation system (red curves).

head.ce:title: Synthesis of carbon dots with tailored heteroatomic structure for achieving ultrahigh effectiveness in persulfate activation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Pei Yang, Ziqi Zhu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are normally feature with zero-dimensional structure, excellent solubility, good biosafety, and exceptional photoelectronic properties, thus, awakening the homogeneous-like photocatalytic potency of CDs in peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation can afford new idea for water body remediation. Herein, we presented a facile nitrogen and sulfur co-doping strategy for the development of high-performance CDs-based photocatalysts. Through the deep investigation on the structure-activity relationship, we proposed that the incorporated pyridinic N within CDs structure could serve as efficient Lewis basic sites for PMS confinement. Importantly, the co-existence of oxidized sulfur groups and specific nitrogen speciation (pyridine N and pyrrolic N) induced unique “push-pull” effect on the photogenerated carriers within the surface state of S,N co-doped CDs. The unique synergy sites and surface hydrophilic nature conferred the CDs exceptional photocatalytic effectiveness, presenting a remarkable PMS consumption ratio of 7.62 per gram of the CDs photocatalyst within just 20 min. Profited from the improved kinetics of interfacial reactions, the CDs-photocatalyzed oxidation system that consisted largely of sulfate radicals can completely degrade rhodamine B within 12.5 min, and hold great potential in long-term operation without needing to regenerate CDs catalyst.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Peroxymonosulfate (PMS)-based advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) have been receiving tremendous attention as efficient remediation approaches for recalcitrant organic contaminants [1–3]. Among different kinds of catalytic strategies for PMS activation, carbocatalysts have been considered as the promising alternatives to get rid of dependence upon precious metal-contained catalysts, due to the merits of cost-effectiveness, acid-base resistance and environmental benign, etc. [4,5]. To this end, various carbonaceous materials including fullerene, carbon nanotubes, reduced graphene oxide, ordered mesoporous carbon, and biochar have been exploited for the persulfate activation by rationally designing their nanostructures [6–10]. Currently, with deepening insights, the intentionally controllable manipulation of electronic configuration and adsorption/desorption behavior is the prerequisite to endow carbon materials with fast mass and charge transfer capability, affordable active site density, and high electrical conductivity. Based on this, increasing researchers devote great efforts to the surface site engineering, involving vacancy defect modulation, non-metallic heteroatom doping, heterostructure construction, and/or covalent functionalization [5,7,11–14], aiming to provide a cost-effective way to synthesize carbon-based catalysts with high activity and durability. Among them, incorporating heteroatoms into carbon-based substrates afford a more flexible springboard to trigger catalytic activities, because non-metallic heteroatoms with different electronegativity (e.g., sulfur, phosphorous, boron, and nitrogen) are conducive to tailoring the local electronic structures of surrounding carbons [15]. Nonetheless, synthesizing the above-mentioned carbon-based catalysts by heteroatom-doping methods cannot get rid of inferior efficiency, and also suffer limited controllability and problem of energy-extensive consumption. More importantly, for either electrophilic nature or nucleophilic nature of surface catalytic sites, most of the developed carbocatalysts catalyze PMS activation by virtue of the redox of surface functionalities, inevitably leading to the deterioration of catalytic performance. Although several post-treatment approaches, such as UV irradiation, organic solvent extraction, and post-annealing treatment are helpful to restore the active sites at some extent [6,16–18], the substantial increase of chemical inputs and operational costs is bound to make barriers for practical wastewater purification. Additionally, catalytic engineering should also promise the scale preparation of carbon-based catalysts for promoting their industrial application. Therefore, developing alternative carbocatalysts with high-performance and mass-producible potential would be highly desirable to overcome the above-mentioned bottlenecks.

Carbon dots (CDs) have aroused significant attention as a new generation of nano-photocatalysts or cocatalysts due to their good light-harvesting properties, environmental friendliness, low cost, and the rich variety of optional functional groups [19–22]. Importantly, compared to heavy metal-based semiconductor quantum dots, the inherent π -conjugated regions could confer CDs-based photocatalysts long-term chemical stability and photostability. Moreover, profited from the synergy of quantum size and hybrid carbon structures, the uniformly distributed catalytic sites on CDs surfaces, which together with the large specific surface area, could enable the fast consumption of photogenerated electrons or holes in photocatalytic reactions, such performance is imperative and beneficial for interfacial electron transfer kinetics and avoiding photocorrosion [23,24]. In this regard, there are some tentative efforts have been devoted to developing high-performance CDs-based carbocatalysts in PMS activation; also, the structure-function relations hidden beneath the surface were deeply investigated to shed light on their viability in practical remediation of water bodies. For instance, Han et al. have confirmed that the surface-contained hydroxyl and carboxylic groups could be essential contributors for the photocatalytic activity of CDs in PMS activation, which fostered the generation of predominant sulfate radicals (SO4 •−) along with minor photogenerated holes in the oxidation system [25]. Strategically, by controlling the oxygen content on CDs’ surfaces, their further research proposed that ketonic groups preferred to promote the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, endowing CDs with remarkable photocatalytic capacity toward PMS activation. In depth, the surface functionalization with phenylhydrazine reinforced the active sites by improving the visible light absorption, which conferred the CDs-catalyzed PMS activation system enhanced oxidation capacity in tetracycline degradation [26]. In effect, incorporating different heteroatoms within CDs structures could facilely steer the electron transfer regime in PMS activation. In our latest study, the nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs far outperformed sulfur-doped CDs in the photocatalytic activation of PMS for degradation of dye pollutant, this was because the synergy of electron-trapping state induced by S-doped species and electron-donating behavior of N-related moieties could offer a strong local electric field, upon which PMS activation was thermodynamically favored via a promoted electron transfer process [27]. Nonetheless, considering the multifarious heteroatoms doping species within CDs structures, the specific contributors of active sites still need in-depth experimental validation.

Herein, we speculate that nitrogen heteroatom doping is able to either create more adsorption sites or confer denser electron-rich sites, and sulfur speciation coupled with specific nitrogen coordination could affect the catalytic performance of CDs due to their largely different electrochemical property (electronegativity: χS = 2.58 vs χN = 3.04) [4,10]. In this regard, by controlling the types of dopants as well as their dosage in precursors, S,N co-doped CDs with different nitrogen constituents, and the CDs solely doped with nitrogen heteroatom were facilely synthesized by microwave-assisted hydrothermal approach. For the sake of gaining deep insight into the structure-function relations, we systematically investigated the influence of heteroatoms speciation within CDs on their chemospecific affinities toward PMS on the premise that the optical properties exert inappreciable effect. Moreover, the effectiveness of CDs in PMS photocatalytic activation was validated by assessing the oxidation degradation of rhodamine B (RhB). As Lewis basic sites, the pyridinic N and pyrrolic N at high doping levels were profitable to the adsorption of PMS. Simultaneously, the opposite electronegativities of the oxidized sulfur groups and N-related species within CDs may confer the surface electron-trapping state “push-pull” effect, making the photogenerated electrons easily available for PMS activation. This high-performance photocatalytic system consisted largely of SO4 •− oxidizing species, showing an efficient oxidation capacity to completely degrade RhB within 12.5 min. Meanwhile, we found that the photocatalytic oxidation system relying on the transfer process of photogenerated electrons held great potential in long-term operation without needing to regenerate CDs catalyst.

2,4-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid (2,4-DASA, >98 %), m-phenylenediamine (m-PD, 99 %), peroxymonosulfate available as KHSO5·0.5KHSO4·0.5K2SO4 (PMS, KHSO5≥42 %), potassium iodide (KI, 99 %), sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4, ≥99.99 %), and 4-chloro-7-nitrobenzofurazan (NBD-Cl, 98 %) were obtained from Aladdin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Citric acid (CA, ≥99.5 %), RhB (98 %), silver nitrate (SN, ≥99.8 %), tertiary butyl alcohol (t-BA, ≥98 %), sodium bromide (KBr, ≥99.0 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥96 %) and acetic acid (CH3COOH, ≥99.5 %) were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3, ≥99.8 %), methanol (MeOH, 99.5 %), and l-histidine (L-his, 99 %) were obtained from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Nanjing, China). 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) was afforded by SigmaAldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). The ultrapure water (Hangzhou, China) was used throughout the experiments. All chemical reagents were used as received without further purification.

The sulfur and nitrogen heteroatoms co-doped CDs (denoted as S,N-CDs1.0), were synthesized by one-step microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. Typically, 1 mmol CA and 1 mmol 2,4-DASA were dispersed in 10 mL thermal ultrapure water. The solution was placed in a reacting vessel and heated in microwave reactor at 180 °C for 10 min. After naturally cooling down to room temperature, the reactants were preliminarily purified by using 0.22 μm filter membrane. Then the resulting mixtures were dialyzed (1000 Da of cut off molecular weight) for 24 h to remove the unreacted precursor. Another kind of S,N co-doped CDs (designated as S,N-CD0.5) were also synthesized following the same procedure except that the dosage of 2,4-DASA was reduced to 0.5 mmol. By adopting the same synthesis procedure, the CDs solely doped with nitrogen heteroatom were also synthesized from the starting materials containing CA and m-PD (with a molar ratio of 1.0).

The photoluminescence (PL) emission and excitation spectra were measured using an F-7000 spectrophotometer (HITACHI, Japan). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorptions of CDs solution were performed on Lambda 950 (PE, American). The absolute PL quantum yield (QY) and the time-resolved fluorescence lifetime were determined by a FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer (HORIBA, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken on a JEM-2100F instrument (JEOL, Japan) operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated by the CDs-catalyzed PMS activation system was analyzed using an A300 electron paramagnetic resonance spectrometer (EPR, Bruker, Germany). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was obtained by using SmartLab SE diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) equipped with Cu Kα radiation at a scanning speed of 5°/min. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured by a VERTEX 80 V spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) with KBr pellet. An X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific, USA) with Al Kα radiation was used to investigate the composition and chemical state of elements on the surface of CDs. The solar light for photocatalytic reaction was generated by Xenon solar simulator (CEL HXF300, CEAULIGHT, Beijing, China).

According to the previous report [28], the photocatalytic effectiveness of heteroatom-doped CDs (75 mg/L) in the activation of PMS (0.714 g/L) was analyzed by measuring the PMS consumption in the photocatalytic system at different time intervals. Specifically, 100 μL sample was taken from reaction solution and then transferred into 10 mL chromogenic reagent consisting of NaHCO3 (0.5 g/L) and KI (100 g/L). Immediately, the solution was thoroughly mixed for a few seconds and allowed to stand for 10 min. After that, the absorbance of reactant was detected using a UV–vis spectrometer at a wavelength of 352 nm.

RhB, as one of the most commonly used dye in textile and paper industries, was selected to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of CDs-based photocatalyst in batch experiments with PMS as oxidant. In detail, the S,N-CDs1.0 were dissolved in 250 mL RhB solutions (8 g/L) with a final concentration of 75 mg/L, then the solution pH was adjusted to 5 with NaOH solution (2 mol/L) or H2SO4 solution (2 mol/L). This mixture was firstly stirred for 30 min in dark to establish adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Subsequently, a specific volume of PMS stock solution was incorporated into the reaction solution to achieve a predetermined concentration of 0.714 g/L. Immediately, Xenon solar simulator (100 mW/cm2) was turned on to initiate the photocatalytic reaction. During the photocatalytic tests, the reaction suspensions were periodically sampled, and the change of RhB concentration was determined by UV–vis absorbance spectroscopy with a detection wavelength of 554 nm, the initial RhB concentration was denoted as C 0, while the variational RhB concentration in the PMS-mediated oxidation process was denoted as C t. As a result, the average values with the standard deviations were obtained by carrying out triplicate experiments. To identify the ROS involved in the PMS activation process, radical quenching analysis was conducted by treating e− with SN, h+ with Na2C2O4, SO4 •− and hydroxyl radical (OH•) with MeOH, OH• with t-BA, superoxide anion radical (O2 •−) with NBD-Cl, and singlet oxygen (1O2) with L-his, respectively [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphologies and surface chemical states of heteroatoms-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 , the TEM images revealed that all the synthesized CDs were well dispersed, with diameters ranging from 1.0 to 5.0 nm. The XRD pattern of each type of CDs showed one broad peak centered at around 20°, corresponding to the interlayer distance of about 0.44 nm (Fig. 2 a), such enhanced interlayer distance in comparison to graphite (0.34 nm) could stem from the heteroatoms doping process [30].

The surface functional groups of CDs were initially characterized by FTIR spectroscopy, as illustrated in Fig. 2b. The stretching vibration of O–H/N–H (3438 cm−1), C–H (2927 and 2850 cm−1), –COOH (1718 cm−1), CC (1625 cm−1) and C–O (1197 cm−1) corroborated that the synthesized CDs were composed of conjugated aromatic structure and O-/N-containing groups. In particular, the emerging of stretching vibration peaks of C–N/C–S (1398 cm−1), CS (1076 cm−1) and SO (1016 cm−1) indicated the extraneous doping of sulfur heteroatom within S,N co-doped CDs [31–33]. Thereinto, the distinct FTIR peaks of C–S and SO bonds revealed the successful incorporation of oxidized sulfur groups on S,N-CDs surfaces by employing 2,4-DASA as the sole dopant. The existence of abundant hydrophilic groups underpinned the excellent dispersibility and stability of CDs in aqueous catalytic system. Furthermore, the differences in surface chemical moieties of each CDs were unveiled by XPS analysis. The XPS survey scans revealed the primary characteristic peaks of O 1s (532.1 eV), N 1s (401.1 eV) and C 1s (285.1 eV) for all the synthesized CDs samples (Fig. 2c). The composition analysis obtained from the XPS characterizations unraveled that, regardless of dopant types and the constituent proportion of precursors, all the synthesized CDs were incorporated with comparable amount of N heteroatom (8.1 % for S,N-CDs1.0, 6.4 % for S,N-CDs0.5, and 10.0 % for N-CDs). In particular, the other two discernible XPS peaks with binding energies at 232.1 and 168.1 eV can be observed in S,N-CDs samples, which were attributed to S 2s and S 2p. Moreover, the atomic percentage of S heteroatom demonstrated a desirable high doping level (3.2 % for S,N-CDs1.0 and 2.5 % for S,N-CDs0.5). For all the synthesized CDs, as shown in Fig. 2d, the analysis on the C 1s spectra validated the existence of multiple functionalities, including C–C/CC (284.1 eV), C–N/C–S (284.9 eV), C–O (286.1 eV) and O–CO (288.5 eV) [34,35]. In addition, the deconvolution of N 1s spectra substantiated the same N-related chemical moieties in the three types of CDs. Specifically, as illustrated in Fig. 2e, the nitrogen-containing groups mainly consisted of pyridinic N (398.4 eV), pyrrolic N (399.6 eV), graphitic N (400.8 eV) and amino group (401.7 eV) [35,36]. The S 2p peak emerged in S,N-CDs1.0 and S,N-CDs0.5 can be deconvoluted into two different components with binding energy at 167.7 and 168.8 eV, confirming that the incorporated S heteroatom existed mainly in the forms of oxidized sulfur groups (Fig. 2f) [27,37,38]. The high-resolution spectra of O 1s displayed in Fig. 2g revealed two conspicuous peaks centered at 531.4 eV and 532.7 eV, corresponding to CO and C–O groups [35,36]. Although the two types of S,N co-doped CDs revealed negligible difference in nitrogen content, the inclusion modes of N heteroatom were distinct upon altering 2,4-DASA dosage in precursor. Quantitatively, the content difference of either pyrrolic N or graphitic N between S,N-CDs1.0 and S,N-CDs0.5 were less than 5 %, while a large gap of pyridinic contents can be detected in the two type of CDs. In detail, the proportion of pyridinic N to the total N-containing species in S,N-CDs1.0 was 21.1 %, whereas the corresponding proportion in S,N-CDs0.5 remarkably decreased to 9.4 % when the 2,4-DASA dosage was halved.

According to the previous report [39], the fusion modes of nitrogen atoms within CDs structures depended on the dosage of nitrogen dopant. In specific, the harsh carbonization process preferentially promoted the formation of N-doped graphitic domains within CDs internal structure when the relative lower dopant was incorporated in precursor. In contrast, nitrogen heteroatom mainly bonded at the edges of aromatic domain when the dopant was superfluous. Taking into account that the distinct inclusion modes of the N heteroatom can differently reshape the local environment of the carbon-based substrates, the altered N-doped configuration in S,N-CDs might come with accelerated separation of photogenerated carriers, which offers possibility to achieve thermodynamically favored PMS activation.

The optical properties involving excitation/emission features, absorption behavior, fluorescence decay, and PL QY were investigated. The investigation on the PL properties revealed that all the synthesized CDs exhibited the typical excitation-dependent emission behavior (Fig. 3 a–c). It is reasonable to deduce that the various N- and S-containing groups induced multi-emissive centers at the surface of sp2/sp3 hybrid carbonaceous core [40]. As revealed by Fig. 3d, in contrast with the N-CDs, a red-shift phenomenon appeared in the optimal emission spectra of S,N-CDs, which could be caused by the fact that the surface-contained oxidized sulfur groups induced an intermediate energy level that below the excited state energy level (π*) of sp2-conjugated system [34]. Despite the different heteroatom-doping species, all the synthesized CDs displayed strong absorption peaks at around 282 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectra, attributed to the π-π* transitions of CC and CN bonds in the aromatic rings [41]. Additionally, a wide band with weak intensity centered at around 436 nm was observed in S,N-CDs1.0 and S,N-CDs0.5 samples, implying the formation of electron trapping state induced by chemical doping of S heteroatom (Fig. 3e). The absolute PL QYs of S,N-CDs1.0, S,N-CDs0.5 and N-CDs were 12.6 %, 19.5 % and 11.3 %, respectively. In general, the PL QY is positively correlated with the radiative recombination efficiency of photogenerated electrons [21,42,43]. In such case, the comparable QYs of S,N-CDs1.0 and N-CDs made a solid conclusion that the incorporated oxidized sulfur groups can hardly contribute to the radiative recombination within the surface state of CDs despite their same N-doping configurations. The time-resolved PL decay curves of all samples were also measured and the results illustrated that they can be fitted by a dual-exponential formula (Fig. 3f). Based on this, we found that the average lifetime of S,N-CDs1.0 (29.4 ns) was much slower than that of S,N-CDs0.5 (8.5 ns) or N-CDs (8.2 ns), Such sufficiently long fluorescence lifetime of S,N-CDs1.0 confirmed the efficient separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, which could preferentially allow the catalytic centers donating photogenerated electrons for PMS reduction.

Herein, the photocatalytic performance of synthesized CDs in PMS activation was assessed by PMS decomposition efficiency under light irradiation. As revealed by Fig. 4 a, thanks to the extremely low usage of CDs in photocatalytic reaction, the characteristic absorption peak of PMS/KI base solution at around 352 nm was free from the interference of CDs’ optical absorption, thus the proposed photometrical method was applicable for monitoring the variation of PMS concentration in our case. As indicated by the imperceptible change of oxidant concentration, the self-transformation of PMS into free radicals seems to be thermodynamically prohibitive (Fig. 4b). Comparatively, the consumption efficiency of PMS catalyzed by S,N-CDs1.0 under light irradiation was more than 80.0 % within just 20 min, while the S,N-CDs0.5 and N-CDs stagnated at pretty low activities, showing PMS consumption efficiencies of 18.1 % and 9.4 %, respectively. According to the previous reports, the catalyst-assisted PMS continuous consumption in the absence of organic pollutants is a typical feature of radical-based AOPs [5,28]. Additionally, the extremely rapid PMS decay signified the facile cleavage of peroxide bonds under the catalysis of S,N-CDs1.0. In effect, the high PMS affinity of S,N-CDs1.0 relying on electron-transfer process favored the generation of oxidizing radicals. Furthermore, in light of the same nitrogen speciation within S,N-CDs1.0 and N-CDs, we can infer that the surface-contained oxidized sulfur groups was indispensable for awakening the photocatalytic activity of functionalized CDs. It is necessary and important to point out that the bottom-up synthesis route could be more available for the high-level of sulfur coordination within the framework of CDs, providing a springboard for tailoring the catalytic activity, while for other carbocatalyst (e.g., graphene, CNTs, or biochar), directly incorporating heteroatom S could suffer great difficulty due to the relative high formation energy within the inert carbonaceous skeletons [10]. To further validate the distinct performance of S,N-CDs1.0 in PMS activation, we compared the catalyzing effectiveness of different carbocatalysts reported in previous researches by evaluating the reduction of PMS concentration (denoted as Δ[PMS]), as illustrated in Fig. 4c. Specifically, for the most of carbocatalysts utilized in PMS activation, the PMS decomposition over per gram of catalyst were rather limited, showing the Δ[PMS]/[catalyst] ratios ranging from 0.49 to 3.14. Meanwhile, the PMS decay spent more than 30 min [7,44–52]. In contrast, the ratio of PMS consumption to the CDs usage in our case was as high as 7.62 within just 20 min, implying the exceptional performance of CDs-based photocatalyst in PMS activation. When considering the distinct advantages of abundant low-cost sources, simple synthetic route, excellent biocompatibility, and low environmental impact, the CDs-based catalysts synthesized by non-metallic heteroatom doping would effectively reduce chemical input and improve economic feasibility for water body remediation. Such overwhelming performance of S,N-CDs1.0-based catalyst in comparison with other reported carbon-based counterparts could be attributed the unique chemical states and electronic structures. For instance, (ⅰ) the preponderance of the surface functionalization or heteroatoms doping lies in a high-density distribution of active sites at the surfaces of CDs; (ⅱ) relying on the photogenerated carriers occurred on CDs surfaces rather than available unpaired electrons to induce PMS activation may be the energetically favorable step, which can get rid of denaturation of catalytic sites; (ⅲ) the surface-contained hydrophilic groups (−COOH and –OH) of CDs are of tremendous benefit to the mass transfer of reactants. In short, both the strong PMS-catalyst interactions as well as the kinetics of photo-induced electron transfer collectively promote PMS activation over the S,N-CDs1.0.

The radical quenching analysis was conducted to identify the generated ROS in S,N-CDs1.0 photoactivated PMS system, as illustrated in Fig. 4d. The photocatalytic oxidation system was capable of completely degrading RhB during a reaction period of 12.5 min, while the RhB removal efficiency dramatically decreased to 18.3 % upon incorporating SN into the oxidation system, which implied that the photogenerated electrons transfer on S,N-CDs1.0 surfaces was the most critical step toward PMS activation. Additionally, we found that Na2C2O4 failed to inhibit RhB degradation, confirming that photogenerated holes were not the direct oxidizing species contributing for RhB removal. The addition of MeOH declined the removal efficiency of RhB from 98.1 % to 31.1 %, and t-BA incorporation exerted 35.6 % inhibition efficiency in comparison to the control over 12.5 min of photocatalytic reaction. The inhibitory effect of NBD-Cl was weaker than that of other quenching agents, leading to only 11.0 % suppression rate during the reaction period. In this regard, the generation rate of O2 •− determined by the reaction kinetics of dissolved oxygen and photo-induced electrons on S,N-CDs1.0 surfaces should be sluggish, which was likely due to the high affinity of CDs surface toward PMS oxidant instead of oxygen molecule. Upon incorporating L-his into the oxidation system, the degradation efficiency of RhB revealed a moderate decrease tendency, showing a removal rate of 76.7 % over 12.5 min, this implied that a spot of 1O2 could be generated in the PMS activation process. According to the above results, we conjectured that the contributions from different ROS to RhB degradation followed the order SO4 •− > OH• > 1O2 > O2 •−. Furthermore, the generation process of OH• and SO4 •− in the PMS-mediated oxidation system was monitored by EPR technique (Fig. 4e). The co-present signals representing DMPO-OH• and DMPO-SO4 •− adducts can be observed in EPR spectra, and the EPR signal intensities were gradually enhanced with prolonging photocatalytic reaction time, indicating the continuous conversion of PMS to SO4 •− and OH• oxidizing species under the photocatalysis of S,N-CDs1.0. Combining with radical quenching results, the S,N-CDs1.0/PMS system involved both the radical pathway and nonradical pathway, thereinto, owing to the efficient cleavage of peroxide bonds by photogenerated electrons, radical-based oxidation pathway was predominant for the dye pollutant degradation. It should be mentioned that the long-term chemical stability and photostability are the top priorities for the photocatalysts. In this regard, the photostability of S,N-CDs1.0 was assessed by continuous irradiation under Xe lamp (λ > 300 nm, 100 mW/cm2). As revealed in Fig. 4f, there was merely a very tiny decrease of PL intensity even after 8 h light irradiation, implying that the catalytic sites on the surfaces of S,N-CDs1.0 could avoid light-irradiation damage effect. The photocatalytic stability of S,N-CDs1.0 was further assessed by replenishing the dye pollutant and PMS at the time interval of 20 min. As a consequence, the removal efficiency of RhB only decreased by 11.8 % after five refills, which confirmed that the S,N-CDs1.0 possessed the potential of long-term operational performance in continuous remediation of actual industrial wastewater.

In our case, the pyridinic and pyrrolic N within the S,N-CDs1.0 could serve as the Lewis basic sites, which were conducive to the adsorption of nucleophilic compounds, such as PMS and aromatic organics [53,54]. Notwithstanding that the heteroatoms speciation within S,N-CDs1.0 and S,N-CDs0.5 showed negligible difference, we found that increasing 2,4-DASA dosage could afford more instinct doping of pyridinic N at the edge of sp2-domains, which could confer S,N-CDs1.0 a higher confinement capacity toward PMS. In general, increasing the bonding sites of catalysts toward PMS favors the electron-transfer process and further the kinetics of free radical generation. Additionally, based on the experimental evidences, the grafting of oxidized sulfur groups on S,N-CDs1.0 surface played vital role in awakening the photocatalytic activity. Given that sulfur doping was incline to tailoring the electron states (charge or/and spin density) of CDs by its different electronegativity from N heteroatom, we conjectured that the specific sulfur and the nitrogen speciation within the S,N-CDs1.0 structure can respectively act as electron donors and acceptors to form an electronic push-pull system, which favored the kinetics of photogenerated electron transfer over CDs surfaces, and further PMS activation for the generation of oxidizing radicals.

In this work, we comparatively investigate the photocatalytic activity of S,N co-doped CDs and N-doped CDs toward PMS activation for organic contaminant degradation. By tailoring the dopant species and usage, the synthesized S,N-CDs1.0, which were possessed of plentiful pyridinic N and oxidized sulfur groups, revealed an exceptional effectiveness in PMS photocatalytic activation. The photocatalyzed PMS decomposition rate over per gram of S,N-CDs1.0 was as high as 7.62 within 20 min, which far outperformed the most of reported carbocatalysts. Mechanistic insights into the photocatalytic PMS activation unveiled that the pyridinic N at a higher doping level within the S,N-CDs1.0 could act as Lewis basic sites that was incline to foster the adsorption of PMS. Additionally, the synergy of oxidized sulfur groups and the nitrogen speciation induced a “push-pull” effect within the surface state of S,N-CDs1.0, which largely contributed to the photo-induced electron transfer upon reacting with PMS. The S,N-CDs1.0 photoactivated PMS system can completely degraded RhB within 12.5 min, in which the oxidizing radicals involving SO4 •− and OH• played the dominant role. Overall, this work has confirmed that the photocatalytic activity of CDs toward PMS can be remarkably improved by tailoring the heteroatom-doping species. Importantly, the customized surface chemical states of CDs provide abundant favorable catalytic sites, which together with photo-induced electron-transfer abilities and excellent water-solubility could render them an exceptional effectiveness in PMS-based AOPs.

Pei Yang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Ziqi Zhu: Validation, Investigation. Ruixin Tang: Investigation. An Shen: Investigation. Wentao Huang: Investigation. Xiaoyan Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [32271784, 32101460], the Postdoctoral Science Foundation of China [2020M681624], the Postdoctoral Research Funding Program of Jiangsu Province [2021K042A], and the Advanced Analysis and Testing Center of Nanjing Forestry University.
